name: Barranquenho

allophone_rules.notes: OVERRIDE of the inherited European Portuguese 'chiado': Barranquenho does NOT palatalise coda /s/ to [ʃ]. Following the Extremaduran/Andalusian adstrate, an internal (pre-consonantal) coda /s/ is aspirated to [h] — mehmu (mesmo), Lihboa (Lisboa), bihtu (visto). Absolute word-final /s/ is deleted entirely, handled pre-lexically by the positional 's' word_final → ∅ map (so no /s/ segment survives for this rule word-finally). Redeclaring the pt-PT rule id replaces it in place (OVERLAY_BY_ID), removing the EP hush. Sources: Navas Sánchez-Élez (2011); Clements, Amaral & Luís (2008); Convenção Ortográfica do Barranquenho (2025) pp. 28–29.

OVERRIDE of the inherited EP voiced hush ([ʒ]): coda sibilants neutralise to the aspirate [h] in Barranquenho, matching the southern-peninsular coda-/s/ aspiration that Barranquenho shares (Andalusian/Extremaduran). The EP chiado [ʒ] is not present. Navas Sánchez-Élez (2011); Clements, Amaral & Luís (2008).

OVERRIDE (neutralising no-op) of the inherited EP dark/velarised coda /l/ → [ɫ]. Barranquenho, under the Spanish adstrate, keeps a clear alveolar [l] in the (internal, pre-consonantal) coda; the strongly velarised Lisbon [ɫ] is not a feature. Word-final tonic /l/ is deleted entirely (Brasí, Natá, Isabé), handled pre-lexically by the positional 'l' word_final → ∅ map. Rewriting l → l cancels the inherited darkening. Navas Sánchez-Élez (2011); Convenção Ortográfica do Barranquenho (2025) p. 31.

Coda-conditioned nasalisation, general Portuguese: a vowel followed by a syllable-final (coda) ⟨m/n⟩ nasalises while the nasal consonant is absorbed — modelled pre-lexically by the positional 'm'/'n' coda→[̃] (combining tilde) map, which deletes the coda nasal and nasalises the preceding vowel by concatenation. This rule supplies the accompanying vowel-quality shift for ⟨a⟩: nasal /a/ raises to [ɐ̃] (ãbu→[ɐ̃bu], campu→[ɐ̃] before consonant), matching European Portuguese nasal-vowel quality. It fires only when the next lattice slot is the nasalisation tilde, so an INTERVOCALIC (onset) ⟨m/n⟩ — which stays a consonant and leaves the vowel oral (lhano→[ʎanu], cama→[kamɐ]) — is untouched. Nasal vowels graphed with coda ⟨am/an⟩: Convenção Ortográfica do Barranquenho (2025) p. 26.

Vowel-quality companion of the coda-nasalisation (see PT_NASAL_A_RAISE): nasal /ɔ/ raises to [õ] before the nasalisation tilde (bom→[bõ], ôi→[õj]), so a stressed open [ɔ] does not surface as [ɔ̃]. Fires only when the following slot is the tilde, leaving oral ⟨o⟩ before an onset ⟨m/n⟩ unchanged. Coda ⟨om/on⟩ nasal vowels: Convenção Ortográfica do Barranquenho (2025) p. 26.
/e/ is raised to [e] before a nasal consonant, including an ONSET nasal that leaves the vowel oral (⟨pequenu⟩ [pɨˈkenu])

ancestors.notes: European Portuguese: the dominant language surrounding Barrancos and the primary structural base of Barranquenho.

Castilian Spanish: Barrancos is surrounded on three sides by the Spanish province of Huelva (Andalusia). Spanish influence is pervasive: betacism (v→b), aspirated h, tch/ch affricate, alveolar trill r (not uvular ʁ). Codified in the Convenção Ortográfica do Barranquenho (June 2025).
Galaico-Portuguese: shared nasal vowels, lh/nh digraph conventions, and general Ibero-Romance vowel quality inherited through the Portuguese base.

notes: Barranquenho, the contact vernacular of Barrancos (Baixo Alentejo, Portugal), spoken by ~1,600–2,000 people. Classified as Extremaduran Portuguese (ext-PT) with heavy Spanish adstrate influence; listed by UNESCO as 'definitely endangered'. PRIMARY SOURCE: Convenção Ortográfica do Barranquenho (II Congresso Barranquenho, June 2025, Câmara Municipal de Barrancos); Gramática Básica de Barranquenho (Gonçalves, Navas & Correia, Universidade de Évora, 2025, ISBN 978-972-778-464-6). KEY PHONOLOGICAL FEATURES: (1) BETACISM: /v/ merged with /b/ — ⟨v⟩ only in proper names/toponyms (Gramática p. 17). (2) ASPIRATED ⟨h⟩ = /h/ (voiceless); also [x] in Spanish loans (e.g. *heringu*, *calheha*) — Gramática p. 18. [ɦ] is NOT documented. (3) CODA ⟨s⟩ → [h] aspiration: *mehmu* (mesmo), *Lihboa* (Lisboa), *bihtu* (visto) — Convenção pp. 28–29. Final absolute ⟨s⟩ is SILENT and NOT WRITTEN in canonical Barranquenho orthography (Convenção p. 31). (4) FINAL -r DELETION: tonic final ⟨r⟩ is silent and not written — *cantá* (cantar), *senhô* (senhor), *dotô* (doutor) — Convenção p. 32; Gramática p. 20. Kept in monosyllable *pur*. MONOSYLLABLE EXCEPTIONS: the Convenção §2.6 Nota keeps the lateral of the monosyllables *mal*, *tal*, *cual* (pronounced in most contexts) and *pur* keeps its -r; these are listed as word_exceptions because the positional layer cannot express monosyllable-vs-polysyllable conditioning (no syllable-count position). (5) FINAL -l DELETION: tonic final ⟨l⟩ is silent and not written — *Brasí* (Brasil), *Natá* (Natal), *Isabé* (Isabel) — Convenção p. 31; Gramática p. 19. Kept in monosyllables *mal, tal, cual*. (6) ALVEOLAR TRILL: word-initial ⟨r⟩ and ⟨rr⟩ = /r/ (not uvular /ʁ/ as in standard pt-PT). (7) CODA-CONDITIONED NASAL VOWELS (Convenção p. 26, §1.8.1 Vocalismo nasal tónico): a vowel before a syllable-final (coda) ⟨m/n⟩ nasalises and the nasal consonant is absorbed — [ã]←⟨am/an⟩ (campu [kɐ̃pu]), [ẽ]←⟨em/en⟩ (tempu [tẽpu], quen [kẽ], plain nasal vowel NOT diphthong [ẽj]), [ĩ]←⟨im/in⟩ (lindo [lĩdu]), [õ]←⟨om/on⟩ (bon [bõ]), [ũ]←⟨um/un⟩ (mundo [mũdu]). An INTERVOCALIC (onset) ⟨m/n⟩ is a syllable onset and leaves the vowel oral (lhano [ʎanu], cama [kamɐ], pequenu [pɨkenu]). Modelled on the nasal CONSONANT: positional_graphemes 'm'/'n' coda (word_final/before_consonant) → U+0303 combining tilde on the preceding vowel (deletes the nasal, nasalises by concatenation); ⟨em/en⟩ retain their own digraph→[ẽ] map. Because ⟨nh⟩/⟨lh⟩ tokenise as single graphemes before this fires, an onset ⟨n⟩ in ⟨nh⟩ never nasalises (banho [baɲu]). Vowel-quality raising ⟨a⟩→[ɐ̃] and ⟨o⟩→[õ] is supplied by allophone_rules PT_NASAL_A_RAISE / PT_NASAL_O_RAISE, which fire only when the next slot is the tilde. (8) NASAL DIPHTHONGS: ⟨-âu⟩=[ɐ̃w], ⟨-âi⟩=[ɐ̃j], ⟨-ôi⟩=[õj] — tonic finals carrying stress (Convenção pp. 26–27). (9) ⟨x⟩ THREE-WAY: initial → [ʃ]; intervocalic in learned words (crucifixu type) → [ks]; other intervocalic → [z]/[s] — Convenção pp. 28, 31; Gramática pp. 18–19. (10) FINAL VOWELS in orthography: unstressed -o → written ⟨u⟩ (*altu, pássaru*); unstressed -e → written ⟨i⟩ (*sociedadi, libri*) — Convenção pp. 20–21. PHONEMES NOT PRESENT: /v/ (betacism), /ʁ/ (replaced by alveolar /r/). CONTACT-VARIETY OVERRIDES (Portuguese base + Extremaduran/Andalusian Spanish adstrate): Barranquenho inherits pt-PT via graphemes_base/allophones_base/positional_graphemes_base, so the three European-Portuguese post-lexical allophone_rules (PT_CODA_S_HUSH, PT_CODA_Z_HUSH, PT_CODA_L_DARK) are inherited by OVERLAY_BY_ID and would otherwise impose the Lisbon 'chiado' [ʃ ʒ] and the dark coda [ɫ] — neither of which Barranquenho has. This spec REDECLARES those three ids to disable them: coda /s/ and /z/ → aspirated [h] (Andalusian-type, NOT [ʃ]/[ʒ]); coda /l/ → clear [l] (no-op, cancels velarisation). Absolute word-final /s/, /r/ and /l/ are DELETED pre-lexically (positional word_final → ∅), the salient southern-peninsular weakening described by Navas Sánchez-Élez (2011) and Clements, Amaral & Luís (2008). SESEO: Barranquenho has no Castilian /θ/ distinción; ⟨c⟩(before e/i), ⟨ç⟩, ⟨s⟩, ⟨z⟩ all map to alveolar sibilants (seseo-like), as in the inherited base — no /θ/ is introduced. VOWEL REDUCTION (decision, per source): the Alentejano base retains the inherited EP unstressed-vowel positional reduction (final -o → written ⟨u⟩ [u], final -e → written ⟨i⟩ [i]/[ɨ] — the raising is orthographically encoded in the Convenção), so the inherited pt-PT vowel positional maps are kept rather than overridden. LIMITATION: fine-grained differences in Barranquenho PRETONIC vocalism vs. Lisbon reduction are not separately quantified in the cited sources at phoneme-mapping resolution and are left as inherited rather than invented (hard rule 9). Barranquenho has NO gold benchmark row; correctness here is by citation, not PER.

sources.author: II Congresso Barranquenho / Câmara Municipal de Barrancos (working group)
sources.title: Convenção Ortográfica do Barranquenho
sources.notes: Primary normative source for Barranquenho orthography and phonology. All grapheme rules in this spec are sourced here unless otherwise stated.

sources.author: Gonçalves, Maria Filomena; Navas, María Victoria; Correia, Victor M. Diogo
sources.title: Gramática Básica de Barranquenho
sources.notes: ISBN: 978-972-778-464-6. Institutional grammar confirming phoneme inventory and stress rules. Affiliations: Universidade de Évora/CIDEHUS-UÉ/FCT/Cátedra UNESCO; Universidad Complutense de Madrid/CLUL.

sources.author: Navas Sánchez-Élez, María Victoria
sources.title: El barranqueño: un modelo de lenguas en contacto
sources.publisher: Editorial Complutense / Centro de Linguística da Universidade de Lisboa, Madrid, 320 pp.
sources.notes: ISBN 978-84-9938-099-5. The reference monograph on Barranquenho as a Portuguese (Alentejo)–Spanish (Extremaduran/Andalusian) contact variety, based on interviews with 30 speakers. Source for the southern-peninsular coda-/s/ aspiration/deletion, /r/ and /l/ weakening, betacism (/v/–/b/ merger) and the non-EP alveolar sibilant/vowel system that this spec models as overrides of the inherited pt-PT allophony.

sources.author: Clements, J. Clancy; Amaral, Patrícia; Luís, Ana R.
sources.title: Cultural identity and the structure of a mixed language: The case of Barranquenho
sources.publisher: Proceedings of the Annual Meeting of the Berkeley Linguistics Society 34, 13–22 (Special session on Pidgins, Creoles and Mixed Languages)
sources.notes: Documents the diagnostic contact phonology: syllable-final /s/ reduced to [h] or deleted (absent from all Portuguese dialects, shared with Extremeño/Andalusian), and word-final /r/ and /l/ deletion. Grounds the coda-sibilant and final-consonant overrides in this spec.

sources.author: Gonçalves, Maria Filomena; Navas, María Victoria; Ferreira, Vera
sources.title: Dicionário de Barranquenho
sources.notes: ISBN: 978-972-778-460-8. Coordinated with Convenção and Gramática under Programa de Preservação e Valorização da Língua e Cultura Barranquenhas.

stress.notes: Barranquenho stress per Convenção Ortográfica (2025) pp. 35–36 and Gramática Básica (2025) p. 12. DEFAULT: paroxytone (penult stress) for words ending in a vowel grapheme — INCLUDING final ⟨-i⟩ and ⟨-u⟩, which are the REGULAR ATONE ENDINGS in Barranquenho orthography (Portuguese -e/-o written as -i/-u). Final unmarked ⟨-i⟩/⟨-u⟩ are NOT oxytone triggers: *pássaru* (stress on first syllable), *pequenu* (paroxytone), *libru* (paroxytone). Only ACCENTED ⟨-í⟩/⟨-ú⟩ (or other diacritic-bearing vowels) trigger final stress: *aquí, azú, cantá*. Oxytone final endings: nasal diphthongs ⟨-âu⟩, ⟨-âi⟩, ⟨-ôi⟩ are inherently tonic finals (Convenção pp. 26–27). Final ⟨-r⟩ and ⟨-l⟩ (tonic) are deleted in Barranquenho canonical orthography and do NOT appear as stress triggers. Portuguese-orthography forms with final -ão/-ãe/-ões do NOT occur in canonical Barranquenho text; the convention writes these as -âu/-âi/-ôi.

location.notes: Barrancos (Baixo Alentejo) — the single border municipality Barranquenho is spoken in. [Wikidata Q368867 P625]
